title: Nomenclature
definition: The rules for turning a formula into a name and back. An ionic compound is named cation-then-anion: the metal keeps its element name — or, for a variable-charge metal, its charge as a Roman numeral (the Stock system, $\text{iron(III)}$) — and the nonmetal or polyatomic ion takes its -ide/-ate name. The formula is the reverse and is pure charge balance: the two ions' charges cross to whatever subscripts make the compound neutral, so $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ give $\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$. The names are sourced conventions; the assembled formula is machine-checked (charge crossover, verified neutral).
